**[STATE OF NEW JERSEY]{.underline}**

**[DEPARTMENT OF LABOR AND WORKFORCE DEVELOPMENT]{.underline}**

**[Division of Wage and Hour Compliance]{.underline}**

**COMPLAINT SUMMARY AND NOTICE TO EMPLOYER**

**Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117**

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Complainant:**                      **Carla Muñoz, Warehouse Associate, Edison, NJ**
  ------------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------
  **Respondent Employer:**              Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.

  **Employer EIN:**                     82-3461097

  **Employer Address:**                 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, NJ 08817

  **Statute at Issue:**                 New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.*

  **Complaint Received by Employer:**   February 3, 2025
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**[Section 1 --- Nature of Complaint]{.underline}**

The above-named Complainant, Carla Muñoz, has filed a complaint with the New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development, Division of Wage and Hour Compliance, pursuant to the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.* (the \"Act\").

Ms. Muñoz is employed by Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (\"Respondent\" or \"Employer\") as a Warehouse Associate at the Employer\'s Edison, New Jersey facility located at 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, NJ 08817. Complainant alleges that on or about a date in late January 2025, she submitted a request to use accrued earned sick leave for the purpose of caring for her domestic partner\'s child, who was experiencing an illness. At the time of the request, Complainant states she had a sufficient balance of accrued earned sick leave hours available for use.

Complainant alleges that her request was denied by her direct supervisor, who informed her that the Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy permits the use of sick leave only for the care of a \"spouse, child, or parent\" and that a domestic partner\'s child did not qualify as a covered family member under the terms of that policy.

The Act defines \"family member\" broadly under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 to include, among other relationships, a child of a domestic partner, a domestic partner or civil union partner of the employee, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, any blood relative, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship. The Employer\'s policy language limiting covered family members to \"spouse, child, or parent\" is narrower than the statutory definition and, as applied in this instance, excluded a relationship --- the child of a domestic partner --- that is expressly covered by the Act.

As a result of the denial, Complainant states she was required to take unpaid time off or use accrued paid time off (PTO) from a separate leave bank in order to care for the child. Complainant asserts that the denial constituted an improper restriction on her right to use earned sick leave for a covered purpose under the Act, namely the care of a family member during illness, which is a use expressly permitted by N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3.

**[Section 2 --- Statutory Basis and Alleged Violations]{.underline}**

This complaint is brought under the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.*, which became effective October 29, 2018. The Act requires every employer in the State of New Jersey to provide earned sick leave to employees and to permit the use of such leave for specified purposes.

**Alleged Violation 1 --- Improperly Restrictive Definition of \"Family Member\"**

The Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy defines covered family members as \"spouse, child, or parent.\" This definition excludes domestic partners, children of domestic partners, civil union partners, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, other blood relatives, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship --- all of whom are included within the definition of \"family member\" under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1. By maintaining and applying this narrower definition, the Employer denied Ms. Muñoz the use of accrued earned sick leave for the care of her domestic partner\'s child, a covered family member under the Act.

**Alleged Violation 2 --- Denial of Earned Sick Leave for a Covered Purpose**

The Employer denied the use of accrued earned sick leave for a purpose expressly permitted by the Act, namely the care of a family member during illness. Pursuant to N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3, an employer is required to permit the use of earned sick leave for the following purposes:

> \(a\) Time needed for the diagnosis, care, treatment of, or recovery from, the employee\'s own mental or physical illness, injury, or other adverse health condition, or for preventive medical care for the employee;
>
> \(b\) Time needed for the employee to aid or care for a family member of the employee during diagnosis, care, treatment of, or recovery from, the family member\'s mental or physical illness, injury, or other adverse health condition, or for preventive medical care for the family member;
>
> \(c\) Absence necessary due to circumstances resulting from the employee, or a family member of the employee, being a victim of domestic or sexual violence, including time for obtaining related medical attention, services from a designated domestic violence agency or other victim services organization, psychological or other counseling, relocation, or participation in related legal proceedings;
>
> \(d\) Time during which the employee is not able to work because of a closure of the employee\'s workplace, or the school or place of care of a child of the employee, by order of a public official due to an epidemic or other public health emergency, or because of the issuance by a public health authority of a determination that the presence in the community of the employee, or a member of the employee\'s family in need of care by the employee, would jeopardize the health of others; and
>
> \(e\) Time needed by the employee in connection with a child of the employee to attend a school-related conference, meeting, function, or other event requested or required by a school administrator, teacher, or other professional staff member responsible for the child\'s education, or to attend a meeting regarding care provided to the child in connection with the child\'s health conditions or disability.

The Employer\'s policy, as described in the complaint and as reflected in the Employer\'s Employee Handbook, states that paid sick leave may be used for \"the employee\'s own illness, injury, or medical appointment.\" This language omits several categories of covered uses required by the Act, including care for family members during illness or for preventive medical care, absences related to domestic or sexual violence, absences due to public health emergency closures, and attendance at school-related functions for an employee\'s child. The denial of Ms. Muñoz\'s request is a direct consequence of these omissions.

The Employer is hereby requested to provide a written response to this complaint within thirty (30) days of receipt. The Division notes that violations of the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act may subject an employer to penalties pursuant to N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7, including fines, administrative penalties, and orders of restitution.

**[Section 3 --- Relief Sought]{.underline}**

Based on the foregoing, Complainant Carla Muñoz seeks the following relief:

> \(a\) Payment for the unpaid time off taken, or restoration of the PTO hours used, in connection with the care of her domestic partner\'s child during the period at issue in late January 2025;
>
> \(b\) Amendment of the Employer\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy to bring it into compliance with the Act\'s definition of \"family member\" under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 and to include all covered uses of earned sick leave as set forth in N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3; and
>
> \(c\) Any applicable statutory penalties authorized under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-7.

The Department of Labor and Workforce Development reserves the right to conduct a broader investigation of the Employer\'s sick leave practices, policies, and records to determine whether additional violations of the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act have occurred.

**HARTWELL & CABOT LLP** *Attorneys at Law*

One Gateway Center, Suite 1200 Newark, New Jersey 07102 Tel: (973) 555-4800 \| Fax: (973) 555-4801 www.hartwellcabot.com

February 21, 2025

*Via First-Class Mail and Electronic Submission*

New Jersey Department of Labor and Workforce Development Division of Wage and Hour Compliance PO Box 389 Trenton, NJ 08625-0389

> **Re: Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117 --- Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.**

Dear Sir or Madam:

**[1. Introduction and Representation]{.underline}**

This firm represents Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. (\"Vantage\" or the \"Company\") in connection with the above-referenced complaint filed by Carla Muñoz pursuant to the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.* (the \"Act\"). The Company received the complaint on February 3, 2025, and this letter constitutes its timely response within the thirty-day period provided.

Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. is a Delaware corporation with its principal offices located at 400 Commerce Boulevard, Suite 300, Edison, New Jersey 08817. Vantage operates a freight brokerage, warehousing, and last-mile delivery business with facilities in Edison and at 78 Doremus Avenue, Newark, NJ 07105. The Company employs approximately 890 individuals in New Jersey, of whom approximately 520 are hourly employees and approximately 370 are salaried. Ms. Muñoz is employed as a Warehouse Associate at the Edison facility and has been in that role for the duration of her employment with Vantage. She is classified as a full-time hourly employee and is eligible for earned sick leave benefits under the Company\'s Paid Sick Leave Policy.

**[2. Factual Background and Company Position]{.underline}**

The Company\'s current Paid Sick Leave Policy was adopted and last updated on January 15, 2022, as part of a comprehensive revision to the Company\'s Employee Handbook. The policy was reviewed at that time by the Company\'s internal Human Resources department, under the direction of Marcus Delgado, Director of Human Resources.

Under the policy, all full-time employees accrue one hour of paid sick leave for every 40 hours worked, up to a maximum of 40 hours per benefit year. The policy states that paid sick leave may be used for \"the employee\'s own illness, injury, or medical appointment.\" With respect to leave taken on behalf of others, the policy permits use of paid sick leave for the care of a covered family member and defines such family members as a \"spouse, child, or parent\" of the employee.

The Company acknowledges that the complaint raises questions regarding whether its policy language fully reflects the scope of the Act\'s requirements. At the time of drafting in January 2022, the Company believed that the family member definition and the described covered uses were consistent with its obligations under New Jersey law. However, in light of the complaint, the Company is reviewing its policy language to ensure alignment with the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act, including the statutory definition of \"family member\" set forth in N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1.

The Company further acknowledges that the policy\'s enumeration of covered uses --- limited to \"the employee\'s own illness, injury, or medical appointment\" --- may not fully reflect the range of permissible uses under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3. The Company is evaluating whether additional covered uses, including the care of family members during illness, absences related to domestic or sexual violence, absences necessitated by public health emergency closures, and attendance at school-related functions, should be expressly enumerated in the policy to ensure that employees and supervisors have clear and complete guidance regarding the permitted uses of earned sick leave.

Regarding the specific incident at issue, the denial of Ms. Muñoz\'s request was made by a front-line supervisor who relied upon the express terms of the written policy. This decision was not made with any intent to deprive Ms. Muñoz of a statutory right, nor was it the product of any animus toward Ms. Muñoz or her domestic partner. The Company\'s HR department, under Mr. Delgado\'s direction, has consistently applied the Paid Sick Leave Policy as written and has administered sick leave requests in accordance with the policy\'s stated terms.

The Company does not concede that a violation of the Act has occurred. However, the Company acknowledges that the complaint raises legitimate questions warranting a thorough review of its policy language and administrative practices, and it is committed to addressing those questions in a diligent and timely manner.

**[3. Remedial Steps Under Consideration]{.underline}**

The Company has engaged Hartwell & Cabot LLP to conduct a comprehensive review of its Paid Sick Leave Policy for compliance with the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act and applicable earned sick leave requirements in all jurisdictions in which Vantage operates. Specifically, the Company is undertaking the following steps:

> \(a\) Reviewing the definition of \"family member\" in the Paid Sick Leave Policy to determine whether it should be expanded to align with the statutory definition under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1, which the Company understands to include the employee\'s child, parent, spouse, domestic partner, civil union partner, grandparent, grandchild, sibling, child or parent of a domestic partner or civil union partner, any other individual related to the employee by blood, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship;
>
> \(b\) Reviewing the list of permissible uses for earned sick leave to ensure that all statutorily required categories of use under N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3 are expressly included in the policy;
>
> \(c\) Evaluating whether Ms. Muñoz should be made whole for any lost wages or PTO hours expended as a result of the denial of her earned sick leave request in late January 2025; and
>
> \(d\) Planning and scheduling training for all supervisors and HR staff at the Edison and Newark facilities on the proper administration of earned sick leave under New Jersey law, including the correct identification of covered family members and permissible leave purposes.

The Company anticipates completing its policy review and implementing any necessary revisions by July 1, 2025, which is the target date for issuance of a revised Employee Handbook covering all Company locations.

**[4. Request for Cooperation and Conclusion]{.underline}**

The Company respectfully requests the opportunity to resolve this matter cooperatively and to demonstrate its good faith efforts to achieve full compliance with the Act. In particular, the Company asks that the Department allow a reasonable period --- through the July 1, 2025 handbook revision target --- for the Company to complete its review, update its policies, conduct supervisor training, and address Ms. Muñoz\'s individual claim before any enforcement action is initiated.

Vantage is willing to provide any additional information, documentation, or records that the Department may request in connection with its review of this matter, including payroll records, leave accrual records, and copies of all relevant policy documents.

For all matters related to this complaint, please direct correspondence to the undersigned at the contact information provided below. Catherine Ng, Partner at Hartwell & Cabot LLP, serves as supervising attorney on this matter and may also be reached at this office. For any corporate-level inquiries, Rebecca Thorsen, SVP and General Counsel of Vantage Logistics Group, Inc., may be contacted directly at the Company\'s Edison headquarters.

We appreciate the Department\'s consideration and look forward to working toward an appropriate resolution.

Respectfully submitted,

HARTWELL & CABOT LLP

By: **\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_**

Daniel Ikeda Senior Associate One Gateway Center, Suite 1200 Newark, New Jersey 07102 Tel: (973) 555-4822

Email: dikeda@hartwellcabot.com

**cc:** Rebecca Thorsen, SVP & General Counsel, Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.

**cc:** Catherine Ng, Partner, Hartwell & Cabot LLP

want to make sure we're handling the notice requirement correctly, especially=
 since CT, NJ, and the new states may each have different rules for when noti=
ce isn't possible.

I'm worried about consistency. If different states have different notice rule=
s, our one-size-fits-all 30-day policy could be creating issues we're not eve=
n aware of.

I know you're buried with the Mu=C3=B1oz matter and the Hartwell & Cabot enga=
gement, so I don't want to pile on =E2=80=94 but I'd rather get these on your=
 radar now before they become bigger problems. Let me know how you want to ha=
ndle. Happy to pull together whatever data you need.

From: Rebecca Thorsen <rthorsen@vantagelogistics.com>
To: Marcus Delgado <mdelgado@vantagelogistics.com>
Date: March 12, 2025, 2:15 PM
Subject: RE: Leave Policy Concerns =E2=80=94 CT Employee Inquiries & CO Early=
 Hires

Marcus,

Thank you for flagging all of this =E2=80=94 I really appreciate you being pr=
oactive, especially given everything that's going on. Quick update on the Mu=
=C3=B1oz matter: the NJ DOL complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117) is still o=
pen. Our response letter went out February 21 but we haven't heard anything b=
ack yet. So we're in a holding pattern there, which is partly why I want to m=
ake sure we're not creating new exposure elsewhere.

Let me take your issues in order.

**CT Paid Medical Leave =E2=80=94 Employee's Own Condition**

My understanding has been that our self-funded paid family leave plan (50% of=
 average weekly wage, capped at $850/week, up to 8 weeks) was designed to cov=
er the family leave component =E2=80=94 bonding, family caregiving =E2=80=94 =
and I assumed the CT Paid Leave withholdings being processed by PinnacleHR So=
lutions meant that employees could separately access the state program for th=
eir own medical conditions. But I'll be honest, I'm not 100% certain how our =
self-funded plan interacts with the state insurance program or whether there'=
s a gap for the employee's own serious health condition.

What I do know is that we have never formally applied for a private plan exem=
ption in CT. Or in NJ or NY, for that matter. I'm not sure whether our self-f=
unded approach is properly structured to serve as a substitute for any of the=
 state programs, or whether it was always intended to sit alongside them.

I think we need Catherine Ng's team at Hartwell & Cabot to look at this as pa=
rt of the broader compliance review we're about to kick off. This may be a bi=
gger structural issue than just CT =E2=80=94 NJ has TDI, NY has DBL, and all =
four of the expansion states have their own paid medical leave programs. If o=
ur self-funded plan doesn't meet the state minimums and we haven't properly o=
pted out, we could have exposure across the board.

**Action item for you:** Can you compile a list of all employee inquiries abo=
ut paid medical leave for their own conditions =E2=80=94 CT, NJ, and NY =E2=
=80=94 going back 12 months? I need dates, employee names, and how HR respond=
ed in each case. This will go to Hartwell & Cabot as part of the engagement.

**CO PTO Payout**

Your CO candidate is likely correct. I recall from law school that Colorado h=
as strong wage protection laws requiring payout of accrued vacation, and I be=
lieve that's still the case =E2=80=94 though I haven't researched it recently=
. Our handbook only addresses CT because that was the only state where we had=
 this issue when the policy was last updated in January 2022. We definitely n=
eed to update the policy for CO, and we should check MA, OR, and WA before th=
ose facilities open.

That said, I do NOT want to hold the CO offer letters. They should go out on =
schedule. But make sure you remove the blanket "no payout" language and inclu=
de a note that the employee handbook is being revised and that the company wi=
ll comply with all applicable state laws regarding PTO/vacation payout. Refer=
ence Colorado law specifically. Don't send letters that flatly state we don't=
 pay out PTO =E2=80=94 that's almost certainly wrong for CO.

This is another item for the Hartwell & Cabot engagement. I'm planning to for=
mally open the engagement with Catherine Ng the week of April 14, 2025, and t=
his will be on the list.

**Notice Requirement**

You were absolutely right to approve the leave. The 30-day notice requirement=
 in the handbook doesn't address unforeseeable leave situations at all, which=
 is a drafting gap we need to fix. FMLA requires 30 days for foreseeable leav=
e but only "as soon as practicable" for unforeseeable leave. I'd expect the s=
tate programs have similar carve-outs. Our policy's silence on unforeseeable =
leave could be read as requiring 30 days in all cases, which would be unreaso=
nable and likely non-compliant.

**Interim guidance:** Please instruct HR staff at all locations that they sho=
uld not deny or delay leave when the need is unforeseeable and the employee p=
rovides notice as soon as practicable =E2=80=94 regardless of what the writte=
n policy currently says. I know that's an awkward position to put people in, =
but we can't have employees being turned away for legitimate emergencies.

The policy language needs to be rewritten to address foreseeable vs. unforese=
eable notice requirements for each state, and this will be part of the handbo=
ok revision targeting July 1, 2025.

**Next Steps**

I want to get the Hartwell & Cabot engagement formalized as soon as possible.=
 All three of your issues are in scope. We have an overall compliance remedia=
tion budget of $175,000, and we'll need to prioritize, but all of these feel =
like top-tier items to me.

Please send me: (1) the list of CT/NJ/NY medical leave inquiries as described=
 above, (2) the draft CO offer letters for my review before they go out, and =
(3) a brief memo on the notice requirement incidents =E2=80=94 who was involv=
ed, how it was handled, and whether any employees expressed dissatisfaction w=
ith the response.

One thing I'm keeping an eye on: the target for the revised Employee Handbook=
 is July 1, 2025, but the MA facility opens August 1, 2025. That's not a lot =
of runway. We need to make sure the handbook is done, reviewed, and distribut=
ed before the first MA employee starts.

We'll get ahead of this.

Rebecca

arly Hires

Rebecca,

Thanks for the quick guidance on all three items =E2=80=94 very helpful. I'll=
 get moving on the action items.

**CT/NJ/NY Inquiry List**

I did a quick scan and have already identified at least seven employee inquir=
ies about paid medical leave for their own conditions across CT and NJ in the=
 last 12 months =E2=80=94 four in Bridgeport (CT), two here in Edison (NJ), a=
nd one in Queens (NY). I'll compile the full list with dates, names, and HR r=
esponses by end of next week.

One thing that came up as I was pulling this together: in the NJ cases, emplo=
yees were also pointed to the voluntary STD through Keystone Benefit Administ=
rators, even though NJ has its own TDI program. I'm not sure our HR staff in =
NJ even know that NJ TDI exists as a separate benefit from our self-funded pl=
an. They've been telling employees to file through Keystone. If the payroll w=
ithholdings through PinnacleHR are going to the state TDI fund, shouldn't emp=
loyees be filing claims through the state?

This might be a bigger misdirection issue than I initially thought =E2=80=94 =
it's not just CT. If HR staff at multiple sites are sending employees to a vo=
luntary, employee-paid STD plan when those employees are already paying into =
state programs that cover their own medical conditions, that's a real problem.

**CO Offer Letters**

Will do =E2=80=94 I'll revise the three offer letters to remove the blanket "=
no PTO payout" language and add a compliance savings clause referencing Color=
ado law. Do you want to review them before they go out, or should I just send=
 them to you after for your files?

One other thing I thought of: should we also check whether our 5-day carryove=
r cap on PTO is an issue in CO? If Colorado requires payout of all accrued va=
cation, limiting carryover to 5 days might also get scrutinized =E2=80=94 it =
could look like we're using the cap to minimize the payout obligation. Not su=
re if that's a real legal concern, but it seemed worth raising.

**Notice Requirement**

I've instructed the Bridgeport HR team to follow the interim guidance on unfo=
reseeable leave. I also checked with the NJ and NY HR leads =E2=80=94 they co=
nfirmed they've generally been flexible in practice when employees have emerg=
encies, but they acknowledged the written policy doesn't give them clear auth=
ority to waive the 30-day requirement. They've basically been making judgment=
 calls without a framework.

That inconsistency concerns me. If one site is strict and another is flexible=
, we could have discrimination claims on top of the compliance issue. The soo=
ner we get the policy language updated, the better.

I'll have all requested materials ready by March 21. One question: should we =
loop in Priya Samuelson at PinnacleHR Solutions on the withholding/state prog=
ram questions? They handle the actual contribution remittances, so they'd be =
able to tell us exactly where the money is going and whether employees are se=
t up to file state claims. Might save some time.

irected to Keystone instead of the state TDI/DBL programs have grounds for co=
mplaints or retroactive claims. The Mu=C3=B1oz complaint is a reminder that o=

ng the vendor to potential issues prematurely. We need to understand our own =

irement, and (2) do not turn away employees who ask about paid medical leave =

"""Build a .xlsx workbook from a JSON spec, applying banker conventions.

Usage: python build_workbook.py spec.json output.xlsx

spec.json:
{
  "sheets": [
    {
      "name": "Assumptions",
      "rows": [
        {"cells": [{"value": "Revenue", "header": true}, {"value": 1000000, "input": true}]},
        {"cells": [{"value": "Growth", "header": true}, {"value": 0.10, "input": true, "format": "pct"}]},
        {"cells": [{"value": "Year 1", "header": true}, {"formula": "=B1*(1+B2)"}]}
      ],
      "column_widths": [20, 15, 15]
    }
  ],
  "named_ranges": {"revenue": "Assumptions!$B$1"}
}

Cell flags:
  input: bool       — blue font (hardcoded inputs)
  formula: str      — black font (computed)
  cross_sheet: bool — green font (references another sheet)
  external: bool    — red font (external link)
  header: bool      — bold, no special color
  format: "currency"|"pct"|"multiple"|"accounting"|None
  bold, italic, underline: bool
"""
import json
import sys
from pathlib import Path

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

# ---------- Sheets 3-9: Per-state detail ----------
def build_state_sheet(name, abbr, data, hc, pay, status):
    ws = wb.create_sheet(f"{abbr} Detail")
    set_col_widths(ws, [20, 30, 38, 42, 11, 50])
    ws["A1"] = f"{name} — Paid Leave Compliance Detail"
    ws["A1"].font = TITLE_FONT
    ws.merge_cells("A1:F1")
    info = f"Status: {status}  |  Headcount: {hc}  |  Annual Payroll: {pay}  |  Gap indicators: RED = non-compliant, YELLOW = at risk, GREEN = compliant, N/A = not required"
    ws["A2"] = info
    ws["A2"].font = Font(italic=True, size=9, color="595959")
    ws.merge_cells("A2:F2")
    ws.row_dimensions[2].height = 26
    # header
    hdr = 4
    headers = ["Category", "Dimension", "Vantage Current Policy / Practice", "State Statutory Requirement", "Gap", "Risk / Notes"]
    for i, h in enumerate(headers, start=1):
        c = ws.cell(row=hdr, column=i, value=h)
        c.fill = HDR_FILL; c.font = HDR_FONT; c.alignment = CTR; c.border = BORDER
    ws.row_dimensions[hdr].height = 24
    r = hdr + 1
    cur_cat = None
    for (cat, dim, vpol, sreq, ind, note) in data:
        if cat != cur_cat:
            cur_cat = cat
            cc = ws.cell(row=r, column=1, value=cat)
            cc.fill = CAT_FILL; cc.font = CAT_FONT; cc.alignment = LEFT_TOP; cc.border = BORDER
            for col in range(2, 7):
                ws.cell(row=r, column=col).fill = CAT_FILL
                ws.cell(row=r, column=col).border = BORDER
            r += 1
        ws.cell(row=r, column=1, value="").border = BORDER
        ws.cell(row=r, column=2, value=dim).font = Font(bold=True, size=10); ws.cell(row=r, column=2).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=2).border = BORDER
        ws.cell(row=r, column=3, value=vpol).font = BODY_FONT; ws.cell(row=r, column=3).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=3).border = BORDER
        ws.cell(row=r, column=4, value=sreq).font = BODY_FONT; ws.cell(row=r, column=4).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=4).border = BORDER
        style_indicator(ws.cell(row=r, column=5), ind)
        ws.cell(row=r, column=6, value=note).font = BODY_FONT; ws.cell(row=r, column=6).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=6).border = BORDER
        # row height heuristic
        ws.row_dimensions[r].height = max(30, 14 * (1 + max(len(vpol)//45, len(sreq)//48, len(note)//55)))
        r += 1
    ws.freeze_panes = "A5"
    ws.auto_filter.ref = f"A{hdr}:F{r-1}"

for (name, abbr, data, hc, pay, status) in STATES:
    build_state_sheet(name, abbr, data, hc, pay, status)

CAT_FONT    = Font(color="1F3864", bold=True, size=10)

CTR         = Alignment(horizontal="center", vertical="center", wrap_text=True)
CTR_TOP     = Alignment(horizontal="center", vertical="top", wrap_text=True)
LEFT_TOP    = Alignment(horizontal="left", vertical="top", wrap_text=True)
THIN        = Side(style="thin", color="BFBFBF")
BORDER      = Border(left=THIN, right=THIN, top=THIN, bottom=THIN)

INDICATOR_MAP = {
    "RED":    ("RED",    RED_FILL, RED_FONT),
    "YELLOW": ("YELLOW", YEL_FILL, YEL_FONT),
    "GREEN":  ("GREEN",  GRN_FILL, GRN_FONT),
    "N/A":    ("N/A",    NA_FILL,  NA_FONT),
}

def style_indicator(cell, ind):
    label, fill, font = INDICATOR_MAP[ind]
    cell.value = label
    cell.fill = fill
    cell.font = font
    cell.alignment = CTR
    cell.border = BORDER

    "2. Vantage's policies are applied uniformly across all states; gaps are therefore evaluated against each state's specific requirements. Where a single uniform policy is compliant in one state and non-compliant in another, each state is scored independently.",

def build_state_sheet(name, abbr, data, hc, pay, status):
    ws = wb.create_sheet(f"{abbr} Detail")
    set_col_widths(ws, [20, 30, 38, 42, 11, 50])
    ws["A1"] = f"{name} — Paid Leave Compliance Detail"
    ws["A1"].font = TITLE_FONT
    ws.merge_cells("A1:F1")
    info = f"Status: {status}  |  Headcount: {hc}  |  Annual Payroll: {pay}  |  Gap indicators: RED = non-compliant, YELLOW = at risk, GREEN = compliant, N/A = not required"
    ws["A2"] = info
    ws["A2"].font = Font(italic=True, size=9, color="595959")
    ws.merge_cells("A2:F2")
    ws.row_dimensions[2].height = 26
    # header
    hdr = 4
    headers = ["Category", "Dimension", "Vantage Current Policy / Practice", "State Statutory Requirement", "Gap", "Risk / Notes"]
    for i, h in enumerate(headers, start=1):
        c = ws.cell(row=hdr, column=i, value=h)
        c.fill = HDR_FILL; c.font = HDR_FONT; c.alignment = CTR; c.border = BORDER
    ws.row_dimensions[hdr].height = 24
    r = hdr + 1
    cur_cat = None
    for (cat, dim, vpol, sreq, ind, note) in data:
        if cat != cur_cat:
            cur_cat = cat
            cc = ws.cell(row=r, column=1, value=cat)
            cc.fill = CAT_FILL; cc.font = CAT_FONT; cc.alignment = LEFT_TOP; cc.border = BORDER
            for col in range(2, 7):
                ws.cell(row=r, column=col).fill = CAT_FILL
                ws.cell(row=r, column=col).border = BORDER
            r += 1
        ws.cell(row=r, column=1, value="").border = BORDER
        ws.cell(row=r, column=2, value=dim).font = Font(bold=True, size=10); ws.cell(row=r, column=2).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=2).border = BORDER
        ws.cell(row=r, column=3, value=vpol).font = BODY_FONT; ws.cell(row=r, column=3).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=3).border = BORDER
        ws.cell(row=r, column=4, value=sreq).font = BODY_FONT; ws.cell(row=r, column=4).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=4).border = BORDER
        style_indicator(ws.cell(row=r, column=5), ind)
        ws.cell(row=r, column=6, value=note).font = BODY_FONT; ws.cell(row=r, column=6).alignment = LEFT_TOP; ws.cell(row=r, column=6).border = BORDER
        # row height heuristic
        ws.row_dimensions[r].height = max(30, 14 * (1 + max(len(vpol)//45, len(sreq)//48, len(note)//55)))
        r += 1
    ws.freeze_panes = "A5"
    ws.auto_filter.ref = f"A{hdr}:F{r-1}"

for (name, abbr, data, hc, pay, status) in STATES:
    build_state_sheet(name, abbr, data, hc, pay, status)

# ---------- Sheet 10: Gap Register ----------

{"content": "---
title: ""
---

::: title
HARTWELL & CABOT LLP
:::

::: subtitle
Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review
:::

::: privilege
PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL — ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT
:::

\

**MEMORANDUM**

+-----------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
| **TO:**               | Rebecca Thorsen, Esq., Senior Vice President & General Counsel, Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. |
+-----------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
| **FROM:**             | Daniel Ikeda, Senior Associate; Catherine Ng, Partner (supervising) |
+-----------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
| **DATE:**             | May 19, 2025                                                |
+-----------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
| **RE:**               | Multi-State Paid Leave Compliance Review — Gap Analysis and Prioritized Remediation Plan (NJ, NY, CT, MA, CO, OR, WA) |
+-----------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
| **Matter No.:**       | VLG-2025-014 (Engagement dated April 14, 2025)              |
+-----------------------+-------------------------------------------------------------+

\

# 1. Introduction and Scope

This memorandum is delivered pursuant to Hartwell & Cabot LLP's engagement letter dated April 14, 2025, under which the Firm was retained by Vantage Logistics Group, Inc. ("Vantage" or the "Company") to conduct a comprehensive multi-state compliance review of the Company's paid leave policies and practices across seven jurisdictions: New Jersey, New York, and Connecticut (the "current states," in which the Company presently operates) and Massachusetts, Colorado, Oregon, and Washington (the "expansion states," into which the Company plans to expand over the next approximately eighteen months). This memorandum accompanies the *Multi-State Compliance Matrix* (separate spreadsheet deliverable) and should be read together with it.

The Company currently employs approximately 2,140 employees across the current states (New Jersey: 890; New York: 680; Connecticut: 570), with annual payroll of approximately $112.4 million. The expansion is projected to add approximately 420 employees (Massachusetts: 130; Colorado: 95; Oregon: 110; Washington: 85) and approximately $23.6 million in annual payroll, bringing total projected headcount to approximately 2,560 and total projected payroll to approximately $136.0 million. The Company's Employee Handbook, including all operative leave policies, was last revised on January 15, 2022, and the Company has targeted July 1, 2025 for issuance of a revised Handbook.

As set forth in the engagement letter, this review addresses five dimensions in each jurisdiction: (i) Paid Sick Leave; (ii) Paid Family Leave; (iii) Paid Medical Leave; (iv) Employer Obligations (registration, contributions, withholding, recordkeeping, posting/notice); and (v) PTO/Vacation Payout on Separation. Federal FMLA compliance, local/municipal ordinances, and workers' compensation are expressly outside the scope of this engagement. Representation in the pending New Jersey Department of Labor complaint (*Carla Muñoz v. Vantage Logistics Group, Inc.*, Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117) is a separate engagement; that matter is addressed here only for contextual purposes and to inform the Company's remediation posture.

# 2. Executive Summary

Our review identifies a substantial number of material compliance deficiencies across all seven jurisdictions. The Company's leave policies were drafted in January 2022 primarily to address New Jersey and New York requirements, with only informal consideration of Connecticut, and they do not address any of the four expansion states. Because the Company applies its policies uniformly across all locations, a single policy construct that is compliant in one state is frequently non-compliant in another. The deficiencies are not isolated; they are systemic and span every leave category examined.

The most urgent findings are fourfold:

1. **Active regulatory exposure in New Jersey.** The Company's paid sick leave policy defines "family member" as "spouse, child, or parent" and limits covered uses to the employee's own health. This is materially narrower than the New Jersey Earned Sick Leave Act ("NJ ESL"), N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1 *et seq.*, and is the direct cause of the pending NJ DOL complaint filed by Carla Muñoz, who was denied use of earned sick leave to care for her domestic partner's child. The NJ DOL has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation of the Company's sick leave practices, policies, and records. This exposure extends to all 890 New Jersey employees.

2. **Imminent state program registration deadlines in the expansion states.** The Company has not registered with the paid leave insurance program in any of the four expansion states. Colorado is the most urgent: three remote Colorado employees were hired in April 2025, meaning the Colorado FAMLI registration obligation was likely triggered at the first date of hire—before the Aurora facility opens on November 1, 2025. Massachusetts is on the critical path: the Worcester facility opens August 1, 2025, and factoring PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc.'s six-to-eight-week payroll configuration lead time, MA PFML registration must be complete by approximately June 1, 2025. Oregon (register by ~December 1, 2025) and Washington (register by ~March 1, 2026) follow.

3. **Structural non-compliance of the self-funded paid family leave plan.** The Company self-funds its paid family leave benefit at 50% of the employee's average weekly wage ("AWW"), capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks. This benefit is below the statutory minimum in all seven jurisdictions—on wage replacement rate, weekly benefit cap, and duration—and the Company has not filed a private-plan application in any state. In Colorado, private employers cannot self-fund in lieu of FAMLI participation at all. Meanwhile, the Company is withholding employee contributions for NJ FLI, NY PFL, and CT PL, meaning employees are paying into state programs that the Company's self-funded plan was purportedly designed to replace. This creates both a compliance gap and a significant employee-confusion and dual-coverage risk.

4. **Paid medical leave misdirection.** The Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee's own serious health condition. Human Resources staff in Connecticut, New Jersey, and New York have been directing employees who inquire about paid medical leave to the voluntary, employee-paid short-term disability ("STD") plan administered by Keystone Benefit Administrators, rather than to the state programs (CT PL, NJ TDI, NY DBL) into which those employees are already paying via payroll withholding. At least seven such inquiries have been documented in the past twelve months (four in Connecticut, two in New Jersey, one in New York). Fewer than 30% of Connecticut hourly staff are enrolled in the voluntary STD plan.

The matrix deliverable scores 301 individual dimensions across the seven states. Of these, 162 are non-compliant (red), 28 are at risk (yellow), 87 are compliant (green), and 24 are not applicable. Every state receives an overall red rating in Paid Sick Leave, Paid Family Leave, and Paid Medical Leave. The remediation work required is significant but achievable within the Company's timeline and budget if prioritized and sequenced against the facility opening schedule.

# 3. Methodology, Sources, and Limitations

Our analysis is based on the following source documents provided by the Company:

- The Employee Handbook, Section 6: Leave Policies (Revised January 15, 2022);
- The internal HR Expansion Plan Memorandum from Marcus Delgado, Director of Human Resources, dated March 28, 2025;
- The PinnacleHR Solutions, Inc. State Leave Program Withholding Summary, data as of March 31, 2025 (expansion-state data as of April 10, 2025);
- Correspondence relating to the NJ DOL complaint (Case No. NJ-ESL-2025-04117), including the complaint summary and the Company's February 21, 2025 response letter;
- The internal email thread between Marcus Delgado and Rebecca Thorsen dated March 10–17, 2025; and
- A representative sample of the Company's manual leave tracking spreadsheets (New Jersey and New York).

We compared the Company's written policies and implemented practices against the statutes, regulations, and agency guidance in effect as of the date of this memorandum in each of the seven jurisdictions. Contribution rates, wage bases, and benefit caps reflect 2025 figures as reported by PinnacleHR and applicable agency guidance. State paid leave law is a rapidly evolving area of regulation; legislative or regulatory changes may occur after delivery that could affect the accuracy or completeness of this analysis. We recommend periodic re-review, particularly as each phase of the expansion is completed and as new legislation takes effect.

This memorandum relies on the accuracy and completeness of the information and representations provided by the Company. It does not constitute a guarantee of any particular outcome with respect to the pending NJ DOL complaint or any future regulatory action, audit, or employee claim.

# 5. Gap Analysis by Severity

We have categorized each material deficiency using the severity framework set forth in the engagement letter: **Critical** (active legal exposure, imminent regulatory deadline at risk, or a deficiency that has already resulted in or is likely to imminently result in regulatory action, penalties, or litigation); **High** (non-compliance likely to result in enforcement action, penalties, or employee claims if not remediated before operations commence or in the near term); and **Moderate** (technical non-compliance, best-practice gaps, or policy ambiguities that may not give rise to immediate enforcement but expose the Company over time or in the event of a complaint or audit). Gap identifiers below correspond to the Gap Register tab of the compliance matrix.

## 5.1 Critical Gaps

**G-01 / G-02 — New Jersey Earned Sick Leave: Family-Member Definition and Covered Uses (NJ).** The Company's Paid Sick Leave Policy (Handbook § 6.2) defines "family member" as "spouse, child, or parent" and limits permitted uses to the employee's own illness, injury, or medical appointment. The NJ ESL Act defines "family member" far more broadly to include a domestic partner, civil-union partner, child of a domestic partner, grandparents, grandchildren, siblings, any blood relative, and any individual whose close association with the employee is the equivalent of a family relationship (N.J.S.A. 34:11D-1). The Act also requires that earned sick leave be available for care of a family member, absences related to domestic or sexual violence, public-health-emergency closures, and school-related meetings (N.J.S.A. 34:11D-3). The narrower policy is the direct cause of the Muñoz complaint: Ms. Muñoz, a warehouse associate at the Edison facility, was denied use of earned sick leave to care for her domestic partner's child. The NJ DOL received the complaint on February 3, 2025; the Company's response was filed February 21, 2025; the matter remains open, and the Department has reserved the right to conduct a broader investigation. This is active legal exposure affecting all 890 New Jersey employees, and the same policy defects (narrow family-member definition and omitted covered uses) are non-compliant in every other jurisdiction examined. *Recommendation:* Amend the family-member definition and covered-uses enumeration to the full statutory scope in the revised Handbook; make Ms. Muñoz whole for any lost wages or expended PTO; and conduct supervisor and HR training on covered family members and permissible leave purposes. Target: July 1, 2025 (Handbook revision), with the Muñoz remediation addressed on an accelerated track.

**G-03 — Colorado FAMLI Registration (CO).** The Company has not registered with the Colorado Family and Medical Leave Insurance ("FAMLI") program. Three remote Colorado employees—a regional operations manager and two logistics coordinators—were hired in April 2025 to perform pre-opening planning work from their homes in the Aurora/Denver metropolitan area, approximately seven months before the Aurora facility opens on November 1, 2025. If the FAMLI registration obligation is triggered by the first date an employee works in Colorado (regardless of whether a physical facility is operational), the Company's registration may already be overdue. Even under the more favorable interpretation that registration is tied to the November 1 facility opening, the six-to-eight-week PinnacleHR configuration lead time requires registration to be complete by approximately September 1, 2025. Critically, Colorado does not permit private employers to opt out of FAMLI in favor of a self-funded plan (the opt-out is limited to local government employers); the Company *must* participate in the state program for Colorado employees. *Recommendation:* Register with the Colorado FAMLI Division immediately; obtain a definitive determination of the registration trigger date for the April 2025 early hires; and provide registration confirmation to PinnacleHR. Target: immediate (April–May 2025).

**G-04 — Massachusetts PFML Registration (MA).** The Company has not registered with the Massachusetts Paid Family and Medical Leave ("MA PFML") program. The Worcester facility opens August 1, 2025, and MA PFML requires employer registration and contribution remittance from the first day any covered employee works in the state. Factoring PinnacleHR's six-to-eight-week configuration lead time, registration must be complete by approximately June 1, 2025—less than seven weeks from the date of this memorandum. Any delay will cascade into a delay in PinnacleHR's configuration, which could result in the Company operating in Massachusetts without proper withholding in place on the facility's opening day. *Recommendation:* Register with the Massachusetts Department of Family and Medical Leave immediately and provide registration confirmation to PinnacleHR no later than June 1, 2025. Target: by June 1, 2025.

**G-05 — Self-Funded Paid Family Leave: Structural Non-Compliance (All 7 States).** The Company's self-funded paid family leave benefit provides 50% of AWW, capped at $850 per week, for up to eight weeks. This benefit is below the statutory minimum in all seven jurisdictions on every material dimension:

| Jurisdiction | Statutory Rate | Weekly Cap (2025) | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| NJ FLI | 85% of AWW | statutory max | 12 wks |
| NY PFL | 67% of AWW | $1,177.32 | 12 wks |
| CT PL | up to 95%/60% (tiered) | ~$941 | 12 wks (+2 pregnancy) |
| MA PFML | up to 80% of AWW | ~$1,170.64 | 12 wks family (26 combined) |
| CO FAMLI | 90% of AWW (tiered) | ~$1,100 | 12 wks |
| Paid Leave OR | 100%/65% (tiered) | ~$1,523.63 | 12 wks (+2 pregnancy) |
| WA PFML | 90% of AWW (tiered) | ~$1,526 | 12 wks family (16 combined) |
| **Vantage (self-funded)** | **50% of AWW** | **$850** | **8 wks** |

Six of the seven states (NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, WA) permit a private-plan alternative, but only if the private plan provides benefits that meet or exceed the state program in *all* respects, and only upon filing and approval of a private-plan application. The Company has not filed a private-plan application in any state, and its current benefit does not meet any state's equivalency threshold. Colorado does not permit a private-employer opt-out at all. Compounding the problem, the Company is withholding employee contributions for NJ FLI, NY PFL, and CT PL—so employees are paying into state programs that the Company's self-funded plan was purportedly designed to replace, while the Company's plan simultaneously falls below those programs. This creates dual-coverage confusion, potential overpayment/underpayment risk, and a basis for employee claims that they are not receiving the benefits they are funding. *Recommendation:* The Company must select a structural approach for each state: (a) participate in the state program and treat the self-funded benefit as supplemental; (b) seek private-plan approval with enhanced benefits meeting or exceeding the statutory minimum; or (c) discontinue the self-funded plan to avoid employee confusion. Colorado requires option (a). This decision should be made promptly because it drives payroll configuration, employee communications, and the Handbook revision. Target: June–September 2025.

## 5.2 High Gaps

**G-06 — New York Paid Sick Leave (NY).** New York requires employers with 100 or more employees to provide up to 56 hours of paid sick leave per year, accrued at one hour per 30 hours worked, with carryover of up to 56 hours and no waiting period before use (NY Labor Law § 196-b). The Company has 680 New York employees and therefore is a 100+ employer. The Company's policy provides 40 hours (full-time) / 24 hours (part-time), accrues at one hour per 40 hours worked, imposes a 120-day waiting period, and provides no carryover. The family-member definition and covered uses are also too narrow. The leave tracking sample confirms that New York employees have been denied use of accrued sick leave during the 120-day waiting period (e.g., Brian Novak, denied January 28, 2025) and have forfeited 14–18 unused hours at year-end under the no-carryover rule. *Recommendation:* Redraft the New York sick leave policy to provide 56 hours (all employees), one-hour-per-30 accrual, no waiting period, 56-hour carryover, and the expanded family-member definition and covered uses. Target: July 1, 2025.

**G-07 — Connecticut Paid Sick Leave (CT).** Connecticut requires employers with 50 or more employees to provide up to 40 hours of paid sick leave per year, accrued at one hour per 40 hours worked, with carryover of up to 40 hours and no full-time/part-time distinction in the cap (Conn. Gen. Stat. § 31-57s). The Company's accrual rate (1/40) is compliant, but its 24-hour part-time cap violates the 40-hour requirement, its no-carryover rule is non-compliant, and its family-member definition and covered uses are too narrow (Connecticut expanded its family-member definition effective January 1, 2025). *Recommendation:* Redraft the Connecticut sick leave policy to provide a 40-hour cap for all employees, 40-hour carryover, and the expanded family-member definition and covered uses. Target: July 1, 2025.

**G-08 / G-09 — Paid Medical Leave Misdirection (CT, NJ, NY).** The Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee's own serious health condition. Employees in all three current states pay into state programs that cover their own conditions—CT PL, NJ TDI, and NY DBL—via payroll withholding administered by PinnacleHR. However, Human Resources staff have been telling employees who inquire about paid medical leave that the Company does not offer such a benefit and directing them to the voluntary, employee-paid STD plan through Keystone Benefit Administrators. In Connecticut, four employees have raised this issue in the past month (including one facing a hip replacement with a six-week recovery and another with a chronic autoimmune condition requiring extended treatment); fewer than 30% of Connecticut hourly staff are enrolled in the voluntary STD plan. In New Jersey, HR staff were unaware that NJ TDI exists as a separate benefit and directed employees to Keystone. At least seven inquiries have been documented across the three states (four CT, two NJ, one NY). Employees are paying into state programs but are not being directed to file claims through those programs. This is a material misdirection that exposes the Company to retroactive claims and to the kind of state-agency complaint already exemplified by the Muñoz matter. *Recommendation:* Train HR staff at all sites on the state program claims processes (CT PL, NJ TDI, NY DBL); correct the misdirection; revise the Handbook language (which affirmatively states the Company does not offer paid medical leave); assess retroactive claims exposure for affected employees; and coordinate with PinnacleHR to confirm employees are set up to file state claims. The Company should compile the full 12-month inquiry list (dates, names, HR responses) as Rebecca Thorsen directed. Target: May–July 2025.

**G-10 — Massachusetts Earned Sick Time (MA).** Massachusetts requires employers with 11 or more employees to provide up to 40 hours of paid sick time per year, accrued at one hour per 30 hours worked, available for use after 90 days of employment, with carryover of up to 40 hours (M.G.L. c. 149 § 148C). The Company's policy is non-compliant on accrual rate (1/40), part-time cap (24 v. 40), waiting period (120 days v. 90), carryover (none), family-member definition, and covered uses, and the Company has not implemented the required workplace posting/notice. These defects must be remediated before the August 1, 2025 opening. *Recommendation:* Redraft the Massachusetts sick time policy and implement posting/notice before opening. Target: by July 1, 2025.

**G-11 — Colorado Healthy Families and Workplaces Act Paid Sick Leave (CO).** Colorado requires all employers to provide paid sick leave at one hour per 30 hours worked, up to 48 hours per year, with no waiting period before use and required carryover, plus supplemental public-health-emergency leave of up to 80 hours (C.R.S. § 8-13.3-401 et seq.). The Company's policy is non-compliant on accrual rate, cap (40/24 v. 48), waiting period (120 days—none permitted), carryover, family-member definition, covered uses, and posting/notice. *Recommendation:* Redraft the Colorado sick leave policy and implement posting/notice before the November 1, 2025 opening (and for the April 2025 early hires). Target: by October 1, 2025.

**G-12 — PTO/Vacation Payout on Separation (MA, CO, OR).** The Company's policy provides for no payout of accrued unused PTO upon separation, except in Connecticut. This is non-compliant in three expansion states: Massachusetts (MA Wage Act, M.G.L. c. 149 § 148, which authorizes treble damages for violations), Colorado (C.R.S. § 8-4-101 et seq., requiring payout of all accrued unused vacation regardless of employer policy), and Oregon (ORS 652.200, treating accrued vacation as wages). The Company's 5-day PTO carryover cap, which forfeits excess accrued PTO, is also likely non-compliant in these states because it effects a forfeiture of earned vacation. A Colorado candidate relocating from a Denver employer flagged the Colorado payout requirement, and Rebecca Thorsen confirmed the Company should not issue Colorado offer letters that flatly state no payout. *Recommendation:* Amend the PTO payout policy to require payout of accrued unused vacation in MA, CO, and OR (in addition to CT); revise the carryover cap; and issue compliant Colorado offer letters with a compliance-savings clause referencing Colorado law. Target: by July 1, 2025 (MA/CO); by January 2026 (OR).

**G-13 — Paid Leave Oregon Registration (OR).** The Company has not registered with Paid Leave Oregon. The Tigard facility opens February 1, 2026; factoring PinnacleHR's lead time, registration should be complete by approximately December 1, 2025. *Recommendation:* Register with the Oregon Employment Department by December 1, 2025 and provide confirmation to PinnacleHR. Target: by December 1, 2025.

**G-14 — Washington PFML Registration (WA).** The Company has not registered with the Washington Paid Family and Medical Leave program. The Kent facility opens May 1, 2026; factoring PinnacleHR's lead time, registration should be complete by approximately March 1, 2026. *Recommendation:* Register with the Washington Employment Security Department by March 1, 2026 and provide confirmation to PinnacleHR. Target: by March 1, 2026.

## 5.3 Moderate Gaps

**G-15 — Oregon Sick Time (OR).** Oregon requires employers with 10 or more employees to provide up to 40 hours of paid sick time per year, accrued at one hour per 30 hours worked, available for use after 90 days, with carryover of up to 40 hours (ORS 653.601). The Company's policy is non-compliant on accrual rate, part-time cap, waiting period (120 v. 90 days), carryover, family-member definition, and covered uses (including safe leave for domestic violence, sexual assault, stalking, and harassment). *Recommendation:* Redraft the Oregon sick time policy before the February 1, 2026 opening. Target: by January 2026.

**G-16 — Washington Paid Sick Leave (WA).** Washington requires all employers to provide paid sick leave at one hour per 40 hours worked, with no accrual cap (employers may cap *use* at a reasonable level but may not cap accrual), available for use after 90 days, with year-to-year carryover of all unused leave (RCW 49.46.200). The Company's accrual rate (1/40) is compliant, but its accrual cap (40/24), 120-day waiting period, no-carryover rule, narrow family-member definition, and omitted covered uses are non-compliant. Notably, Washington also requires payout of accrued unused paid sick leave at separation (it is treated as wages)—the Company's no-payout rule is non-compliant on this point as well. *Recommendation:* Redraft the Washington sick leave policy (no accrual cap, 90-day use threshold, unlimited carryover, expanded uses, payout at separation) and implement posting/notice before the May 1, 2026 opening. Target: by April 2026.

**G-17 — Paid Family Leave Notice Requirement (All 7 States).** The Company's policy requires 30 calendar days' advance notice for all paid family leave and is silent on unforeseeable leave. This creates a denial risk for legitimate emergencies and is non-compliant in states whose programs require only "as soon as practicable" notice for unforeseeable leave. A Connecticut employee recently gave 10 days' notice before starting paid family leave to care for a parent after an unexpected hospitalization; HR initially pushed back citing the 30-day rule, and the leave was approved only by ad-hoc manager intervention. Inconsistent application across sites (some strict, some flexible) creates a disparate-treatment/discrimination risk on top of the compliance issue. *Recommendation:* Rewrite the notice provision to distinguish foreseeable leave (30 days) from unforeseeable leave (as soon as practicable) consistent with each state's rules; the interim guidance Rebecca Thorsen issued (do not deny or delay leave for unforeseeable situations) should remain in effect until the policy is revised. Target: July 1, 2025.

**G-18 — Medical Documentation Threshold (All 7 States).** The Company requires a doctor's note after two consecutive scheduled workdays. Several states (NJ, NY, WA) permit an employer to require documentation only after three or more consecutive days; the Company's two-workday trigger is more frequent than those statutes allow and is potentially over-burdensome. *Recommendation:* Align the documentation threshold to the most protective state rule (three consecutive days) across all jurisdictions. Target: July 1, 2025.

**G-19 — Workplace Posting / Individual Notice (All 7 States).** Every jurisdiction examined imposes workplace posting and/or individual employee notice obligations for paid sick leave and/or paid family/medical leave. The Handbook does not address these obligations, and the Company has not confirmed compliant postings at its current facilities. *Recommendation:* Audit postings at all current facilities and implement compliant postings/notices in each expansion state before the applicable facility opening. Target: May–July 2025 (current states); before each expansion opening.

**G-20 — Manual Leave Tracking System (All 7 States).** The Company tracks sick leave accruals, usage, and balances through manual Microsoft Excel workbooks maintained by two HR coordinators, one workbook per state. The system has no state-specific rule differentiation, no automated alerts (for accrual caps, waiting-period eligibility, or carryover deadlines), no audit trail, and no integration with the Company's HRIS (WorkBridge Pro) or time-and-attendance system (ClockSync Enterprise). Recent internal audits have uncovered errors, including: a part-time New Jersey employee's sick leave balance capped at 24 hours due to application of the wrong accrual rate; negative balances (e.g., James Kowalski at −2.5 hours; Hassan Ali at −0.75 hours); employees forfeiting 8–18 unused hours at year-end under the no-carryover rule; and the 120-day waiting period applied uniformly without confirmation of consistency with state law. The system tracks only earned sick leave—not paid family leave or PTO—and paid family leave requests are tracked through a separate paper-based process. Scaling from three workbooks to seven, with each new state adding distinct accrual rules, carryover provisions, and reporting obligations, is not sustainable and poses recordkeeping-compliance risk (several expansion states impose specific recordkeeping requirements). *Recommendation:* Procure and implement a dedicated leave management software solution integrated with WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync Enterprise, configured with state-specific rules for all seven jurisdictions. Target: vendor selection by July 1, 2025; system live by Q1 2026.

**G-21 — Military Exigency (NY, CT, WA).** The Company's paid family leave policy does not cover qualifying military exigency, which is a qualifying event under NY PFL, CT PL, and WA PFML. *Recommendation:* Add qualifying military exigency as a covered reason in the revised policy. Target: July 1, 2025.

**G-22 — Connecticut PTO Carryover Cap (CT).** Connecticut treats accrued vacation as wages; the Company's 5-day carryover cap, which forfeits excess accrued PTO, may be scrutinized under Connecticut law. *Recommendation:* Review the carryover cap for consistency with Connecticut's wage treatment of vacation. Target: July 1, 2025.

**G-23 — Outdated Employee Handbook (All 7 States).** The Handbook (Rev. January 15, 2022) predates all four expansion states and does not address their requirements. The Company has targeted July 1, 2025 for a revised Handbook—one month before the Massachusetts facility opens. *Recommendation:* Complete the multi-state Handbook revision by July 1, 2025, allowing time for distribution and acknowledgment collection before the first expansion-state employees are onboarded. Target: July 1, 2025.

The Company's failure to register with the paid leave insurance programs in any of the four expansion states is the single most time-sensitive operational issue in this engagement. PinnacleHR has confirmed that it cannot initiate withholding or premium remittance in any state until the Company completes registration and provides registration confirmation and employer account numbers, and that its lead time for configuring withholding following receipt of registration confirmation is approximately six to eight weeks. The registration deadlines, back-calculated from each facility's opening date and PinnacleHR's lead time, are as follows:

| State | Program | Facility Opening | Registration Deadline (est.) | Status | Urgency |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Colorado | CO FAMLI | Nov 1, 2025 | ~Sept 1, 2025 (or earlier — early hires Apr 2025) | NOT REGISTERED | CRITICAL |
| Massachusetts | MA PFML | Aug 1, 2025 | ~June 1, 2025 | NOT REGISTERED | CRITICAL |
| Oregon | Paid Leave Oregon | Feb 1, 2026 | ~Dec 1, 2025 | NOT REGISTERED | HIGH |
| Washington | WA PFML | May 1, 2026 | ~Mar 1, 2026 | NOT REGISTERED | HIGH |

**Colorado requires special attention.** Three remote Colorado employees were hired in April 2025. If the FAMLI registration obligation attaches upon the first date a Colorado employee performs work (the more defensible reading of the statute), the Company's registration was likely due in April 2025 and may already be overdue. The Company should obtain a definitive determination of the trigger date and register immediately. Because Colorado does not permit private employers to self-fund in lieu of FAMLI, there is no alternative to state-program participation.

**Massachusetts is on the critical path.** The June 1, 2025 deadline leaves less than seven weeks from the date of this memorandum. Any delay will cascade into PinnacleHR's configuration and risks the Company operating in Massachusetts without proper withholding on the facility's opening day. As flagged in the engagement letter, the Firm is prepared to issue an early-action notification on Massachusetts registration in advance of the interim status update if circumstances warrant.

For each expansion state, the Company must also determine whether to participate in the state program or apply for a private-plan exemption (where available). PinnacleHR can configure withholding for either approach once the Company provides direction and registration/approval documents. As noted in Section 5.1, Colorado offers no private-employer opt-out; the other three expansion states permit private plans only if benefits meet or exceed the state program, which the Company's current self-funded plan does not.

# 7. Self-Funded Paid Family Leave — Structural Analysis

The Company's self-funded paid family leave benefit (50% of AWW, capped at $850/week, for up to eight weeks) was established as an alternative to participation in state-administered insurance programs. The Company has represented that it "believes that its self-funded paid family leave benefit meets or exceeds the requirements of applicable state paid family leave programs." Our review does not support that belief. As detailed in Section 5.1 (G-05), the benefit is below the statutory minimum in all seven jurisdictions on rate, cap, and duration, and no private-plan application has been filed in any state.

The structural problem is compounded by the contribution structure. PinnacleHR is withholding employee contributions for NJ FLI, NY PFL, and CT PL. Employees are therefore paying into state programs that the self-funded plan was purportedly designed to replace, while the self-funded plan simultaneously falls below those programs. Employees in Connecticut have already expressed confusion about whether they are covered by the state program or the Company's benefit, and at least two have asked whether the Company's benefit provides the same level of coverage as the state program. This dual-coverage posture creates overpayment/underpayment risk and a basis for employee claims.

The Company should select one of the following approaches for each state, in coordination with PinnacleHR and the Firm:

- **(a) Participate in the state program and treat the self-funded benefit as supplemental.** This is the lowest-risk approach and is the *only* available approach in Colorado. Employees receive the full state benefit; the Company's self-funded benefit can be layered on top (or discontinued to avoid confusion).
- **(b) Seek private-plan approval with enhanced benefits.** Available in NJ, NY, CT, MA, OR, and WA, but only if the plan is enhanced to meet or exceed the state program in all respects (rate, cap, duration, covered leave types) and a formal application is filed and approved. This requires a meaningful increase in the benefit level.
- **(c) Discontinue the self-funded plan.** Eliminates employee confusion and dual-coverage risk; employees rely on the state programs (plus voluntary STD for own conditions where applicable).

We recommend that the Company make this decision promptly, because it drives payroll configuration, employee communications, and the Handbook revision. The decision for Colorado is foreclosed (mandatory participation). For Massachusetts, the decision should be made in conjunction with the June 1, 2025 registration.

# 8. Paid Medical Leave / STD Misdirection Analysis

The Company provides no employer-funded paid medical leave for an employee's own serious health condition. In the current states, employees pay into state programs that cover their own conditions—CT PL, NJ TDI, and NY DBL—via PinnacleHR withholding. In the expansion states, the state PFML/FAMLI/PLO programs cover the employee's own serious health condition, and the Company will be required to participate (or obtain an approved private plan).

The operational defect is that HR staff have been directing employees who inquire about paid medical leave to the voluntary, employee-paid STD plan through Keystone Benefit Administrators, rather than to the state programs. This misdirection has occurred in all three current states and has affected at least seven employees in the past twelve months. The Handbook language affirmatively states that the Company "does NOT provide employer-funded paid medical leave," which, while technically accurate as to the Company's own benefit, is misleading in context because it does not inform employees of their state-program entitlements. Fewer than 30% of Connecticut hourly staff are enrolled in the voluntary STD plan, meaning the majority of employees who were directed to Keystone had no coverage at all for their own conditions unless they independently pursued a state claim.

This pattern is the kind of practice that led an employee (Ms. Muñoz) to file a state-agency complaint in the sick-leave context. The Company should treat the medical-leave misdirection as a priority remediation item: train HR staff on state program claims processes, correct the misdirection, revise the Handbook language, assess retroactive claims exposure, and coordinate with PinnacleHR to confirm employees are set up to file state claims. Rebecca Thorsen has directed Marcus Delgado to compile the full 12-month inquiry list (dates, names, HR responses), which should be provided to the Firm to support the exposure assessment.

# 9. Manual Leave Tracking System — Operational Risk

The Company's manual, spreadsheet-based leave tracking system is adequate for three states with relatively consistent policies but is not scalable to seven states with materially different accrual rules, carryover provisions, waiting periods, and reporting obligations. The specific deficiencies—no state-specific rule differentiation, no automated alerts, no audit trail, no system integration, and limited scope (sick leave only)—are detailed in Section 5.3 (G-20). The documented errors (part-time accrual miscalculation, negative balances, year-end forfeitures, and uniform application of the 120-day waiting period without state-law verification) illustrate the systemic weakness.

Several expansion states impose specific recordkeeping requirements regarding leave accruals and usage. A manual system with no audit trail will not withstand a regulatory audit or employee complaint—particularly given the active NJ DOL complaint, in which the Department has reserved the right to investigate the Company's records broadly. We concur with Mr. Delgado's recommendation that the Company invest in a dedicated leave management software solution integrated with WorkBridge Pro and ClockSync Enterprise, configured with state-specific rules for all seven jurisdictions. Mr. Delgado estimates the cost at $40,000–$75,000 and the implementation timeline at three to six months. This investment should be sequenced so that the system is live and configured for all seven states by Q1 2026, in time to support the Oregon and Washington facility launches. In the interim, the manual process should be supplemented with documented state-specific rule sheets, a standardized audit-log practice, and monthly reconciliation reviews.
